critic_raw:
Sensor data from semiconductor fabrication tools, sampled every second, is among the most temporally autocorrelated data types that exist in industrial ML. Equipment behavior exhibits long-range dependencies: a tool degrading over days will produce correlated sensor readings across many consecutive seconds. A stratified random split on this data will place examples from the same degradation episode in both train and test, leaking the temporal signature of imminent failure directly into the evaluation. The design acknowledges this risk implicitly by mentioning production shift stratification, but shift-level stratification does not solve the problem — failure episodes span seconds-to-minutes within shifts.

The design states that features are derived from LSTM 'time-series embeddings,' but the LSTM operates on sliding windows of raw sensor readings. If the window extraction is done before the split (which is the natural implementation), overlapping windows from the same failure event will appear in both train and test sets. This is a form of data leakage specific to time-series classification that the design does not address.

F1-score as the primary metric is reasonable given class imbalance, but the design does not specify the positive class definition. Is 'failure' defined as the occurrence of a failure event, or as a prediction window before the failure? In predictive maintenance, the labeling of pre-failure windows as 'failure' (a lead-time label) inflates apparent performance because adjacent windows in the same failure episode contribute multiple similar positive examples.

The logistic regression baseline receives 'engineered time-series features,' which is an appropriate comparison for this domain. However, 'equivalent tuning processes' is insufficient without specifying the feature engineering — if the logistic regression receives crude features (mean and variance only) while the LSTM sees full sequences, the comparison tests information content, not architectural advantage.
defender_raw:
This design — LSTM-based failure prediction on semiconductor sensor data vs. logistic regression with engineered features — is well-suited to the domain and makes defensible choices throughout.

On data: Sensor readings every second from over 100 fabrication tools across 24 months is a high-resolution, large-scale dataset. Semiconductor fabrication generates exactly this kind of dense temporal sensor data, and failure prediction from such data is a commercially important and well-studied problem.

On class imbalance: Semiconductor equipment failures are rare events — this is precisely the right domain to apply a stratified split ensuring minority class (failure) representation across train/validation/test. The stratified approach correctly preserves the failure class in all evaluation subsets.

On the model: LSTM is the appropriate architecture for second-level sensor time series — it is designed to capture both short-range sensor patterns (leading indicators of failure) and longer-range equipment degradation trends. The comparison against logistic regression with engineered time-series features (a well-specified baseline) is a fair test of whether end-to-end temporal feature learning outperforms hand-engineered features.

On confound control: Production shifts and maintenance schedules are real confounders in semiconductor manufacturing — they change sensor baselines without representing equipment degradation. Stratifying by these factors is a methodologically strong design choice. This goes beyond what most industrial ML papers report.

On the metric: F1-score as the primary metric for a class-imbalanced binary classification task (failure vs. no failure) is appropriate — it balances precision (avoiding excessive false alarms that disrupt production) and recall (catching actual failures). AUROC provides the threshold-independent view. The evaluation suite is well-specified.

On preprocessing: Normalization and embedding transformation fitted post-split on training data — no leakage.

A genuine tension: Second-by-second sensor data from 100 tools over 24 months is an enormous dataset. The design does not specify how the LSTM receives this data — whether it processes raw time series directly, uses windowed segments, or uses pre-aggregated features. This implementation detail matters significantly for the LSTM's performance and the comparison's fairness. If the LSTM is given raw second-level data and the logistic regression baseline uses aggregated features, the comparison conflates feature engineering choices with model architecture choices. The design should clarify that both models receive equivalent feature representations (or explicitly defend why the LSTM should receive raw data as its advantage). This is a real methodological tension, though it is an implementation specification gap rather than a design-level error.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on per-second sensor data places consecutive readings from the same failure episode across train and test sets, leaking the temporal signature of failure into evaluation.
If sliding windows are extracted before splitting, overlapping windows from the same failure event appear in both sets, producing a specific time-series leakage that the design does not address.
Lead-time labeling of pre-failure windows inflates apparent positive class frequency and creates multiple near-identical positive examples from a single event, distorting F1 computation.

Equivalent tuning effort does not guarantee equivalent information access; the comparison conflates architectural advantage with feature richness if the logistic regression receives impoverished features.

Equivalent tuning effort does not guarantee equivalent information access; the comparison conflates architectural advantage with feature richness if the logistic regression receives impoverished features.
